description: Question-led source-of-truth model for a GreenOps data input maturity assessment and reporting tool. Designed for derived scoring rather than direct self-selected maturity.

1.label: Initial / Ad hoc
1.description: Inputs are fragmented, reactive, estimate-heavy and not reliable enough for confident decision-making.

2.label: Managed / Repeatable
2.description: Inputs can be gathered and reused in a repeatable way, but coverage, precision and attribution remain inconsistent.

3.label: Defined / Standardised
3.description: Data sources, methods and ownership are documented and standardised across key areas.

4.label: Quantitatively Managed / Measured
4.description: Data quality, granularity, coverage and confidence are measured and controlled using evidence.

5.label: Optimising
5.description: Inputs are continuously improved, increasingly automated and embedded into operational and strategic decision-making.

1.label: Reporting only
1.description: Data is suitable mainly for broad directional reporting and high-level awareness.

2.label: Directional decision support
2.description: Data can support indicative prioritisation but not high-confidence operational decisions.

3.label: Controlled decision-grade
3.description: Data is sufficiently reliable for controlled operational and management decisions in defined areas.

4.label: Optimisation-grade
4.description: Data is sufficiently granular, timely and attributable to support continuous optimisation and stronger automation.

global_scoring_rules.primary_principle: Domain maturity is calculated from weighted question responses, not directly selected by the user as the primary assessment method.
global_scoring_rules.derived_score_logic: Calculate weighted average score per domain using question weights. Convert to integer maturity band using nearest band with upward rounding from .75.
global_scoring_rules.direct_manual_override_policy: If manual override is allowed, it must be optional, secondary, clearly labelled as assessor override, and the tool must preserve both calculated and overridden scores.
global_scoring_rules.confidence_guidance: Confidence should reflect evidence quality, consistency of answers, clarity of lineage and completeness of rationale.
global_scoring_rules.impact_guidance: Impact is a separate score reflecting materiality, decision relevance, dependency value and risk if weak.

domains.name: Asset inventory and configuration data
domains.definition: Assesses whether the technology estate is known well enough to support GreenOps calculations across cloud, on-premise, end-user and colocation environments.
domains.why_it_matters: Weak inventory data undermines embodied emissions, service mapping, lifecycle decisions, utilisation analysis and attribution.
recommendation_themes: Link assets to services, owners and lifecycle state

scoring.normalisation: weighted average of selected option scores (1-5), rounded to nearest whole number with .75 thresholds upward
question_override_rules: If more than 30% of answers are level 1, overall maturity cannot exceed 3 unless assessor override is applied.
If evidence coverage is weak across more than half of weighted questions, confidence should be reduced.
If assurance/lineage questions score 1 or 2, decision-readiness should be capped at directional support unless other evidence is strong and override is used.

confidence_logic.low: Sparse evidence, inconsistent notes, or material contradictions across answers.
confidence_logic.medium: Reasonable evidence coverage with some gaps or assumptions.
confidence_logic.high: Strong evidence coverage, consistent answers, clear ownership, documented methods.

questions.text: Do you have a reliable inventory of IT assets across all your environments?
questions.evidence_hint: Inventory coverage report, asset counts by domain

options.label: No reliable inventory. Fragmented spreadsheets, outdated records, or no central view of IT assets.
Basic CMDB or spreadsheet covers main platforms but is incomplete, manually maintained and often out of date.
CMDB covers most on-premise and cloud assets with periodic discovery scans. Some environments still missing.
Integrated CMDB with automated discovery across on-premise, cloud and edge. Coverage gaps are known and tracked.
Real-time, continuously validated asset inventory across all environments with automated reconciliation and drift detection.

questions.text: How detailed is the asset data you hold?

options.label: Asset records are minimal — maybe hostname, location and rough category. No configuration detail.
Core fields like make, model, CPU, memory for major assets. Gaps in storage, networking and end-user devices.
Good configuration detail for servers and storage. Networking and end-user device data improving.
Detailed configuration, firmware, warranty and support data for most asset classes. Linked to vendor product catalogues.
Comprehensive, validated configuration data across all asset classes. Automatically enriched from vendor APIs and discovery tools.

questions.text: Can you link assets to the services, applications, platforms or teams that use them?

options.label: No meaningful link between assets and the services, applications or teams that use them.
Some assets linked to major applications or platforms. Mostly manual and incomplete.
Most server and storage assets mapped to services or applications. Cloud resources tagged to teams for main accounts.
Strong asset-to-service mapping across most platforms. Ownership, cost centre and application links maintained.
Full traceability from asset through to service, application, business owner and cost centre. Continuously validated.

questions.text: How quickly does the inventory reflect changes such as deployments, moves or removals?

options.label: Inventory is rarely updated. Changes accumulate and are caught in periodic audits or not at all.
Updates happen during scheduled refresh cycles or when problems arise. Lag of weeks to months.
Main environments updated within days of changes. Some environments still lag.
Most changes reflected within 24-48 hours through automated discovery and integration with change management.
Near-real-time. Deployments, moves and removals reflected automatically through CI/CD, ITSM and discovery integration.

questions.text: Can you tell where assets are actually deployed?

options.label: Location data is unreliable or missing for most assets.
Broad location known (site, building) for major assets. Specific rack, room or cloud region often unknown.
Site and room or zone-level location for most on-premise assets. Cloud region known for main accounts.
Rack-level location for on-premise, availability zone for cloud. Accurate across most of the estate.
Precise location (site, room, rack, position) for physical assets and region/zone for cloud. Automatically maintained.

questions.text: How easy is it to match your inventory to product data needed for GreenOps calculations?

options.label: Cannot match inventory to vendor product data for GreenOps calculations.
Can match a few major product families manually. Most assets lack the detail needed for emissions lookups.
Can match most servers and storage to vendor product data. Gaps in networking, peripherals and end-user devices.
Strong match rates across main asset classes. Product-specific data available for embodied carbon and power modelling.
Automated matching to vendor product catalogues. Embodied carbon, rated power and lifecycle data linked at asset level.

questions.text: Can you explain where your inventory data comes from and how trustworthy it is?

options.label: Data sources unclear. No documented provenance or quality assessment.
Main sources known (e.g. CMDB, procurement) but data quality and freshness vary and are not tracked.
Sources documented for main asset classes. Some quality metrics tracked. Known gaps acknowledged.
Clear data lineage from source through to reporting. Quality metrics tracked with regular audit.
Full provenance with confidence scoring. Automated quality monitoring, exception tracking and source validation.

questions.text: Do you track lifecycle status properly, for example in use, spare, due for refresh, retired or awaiting disposal?

options.label: Lifecycle status not tracked. Cannot distinguish in-use from spare, retired or awaiting disposal.
Some lifecycle tracking for major assets — mainly procurement and disposal dates. Status often out of date.
Lifecycle status maintained for most assets. Refresh planning data available but not always current.
Active lifecycle management with status, age, refresh eligibility and disposal tracking across most asset classes.
Full lifecycle governance: status, age, warranty, refresh plan, reuse potential and disposal route tracked and actively managed.

recommendation_triggers.guidance: Prioritise foundational improvement in asset inventory and configuration data before making strong decision-grade claims.
Standardise and expand asset inventory and configuration data so it supports more controlled decisions.
Use stronger asset inventory and configuration data capability to drive optimisation and measurable intervention, not just reporting.

domains.name: Operational power and energy data
domains.definition: Assesses the quality, granularity and usability of operational electricity and energy inputs across on-premise, colocation and, where possible, cloud-related models.
domains.why_it_matters: Energy is the core activity driver for many GreenOps calculations. Weak energy data limits reporting, overhead allocation, optimisation and hotspot detection.
recommendation_themes: Replace spend-based estimates with measured energy
Increase granularity from site to rack/host/workload where justified
Strengthen attribution to services and workloads

questions.text: What do you mainly use today to work out operational energy use for on-premise and colocation environments?

options.label: Mostly estimates based on utility bills, contract values, floor space or broad assumptions.
Site-level utility meter data for main facilities. Estimates or provider invoices for others.
Measured site-level energy data for most key environments. Sub-metering available in some facilities.
Sub-metered data at hall, floor or cluster level for main sites. Smart meter telemetry where available.
Granular, metered energy data linked to IT load at rack, PDU or circuit level across the estate.

questions.text: How detailed is your energy data in practice?

options.label: Only total site or building-level figures. No breakdown by area, function or IT load.
Site totals with a rough split between IT and facilities. No detailed breakdown within IT.
IT load separated from cooling and facilities for main sites. Some hall-level or floor-level breakdown.
Detailed breakdown: IT load, cooling, lighting, UPS losses separately metered for main facilities.
Granular breakdown to rack row, PDU or circuit level. IT load, cooling, losses and renewables all separately visible.

questions.text: How much of the estate is covered by measured or modelled energy data?

options.label: Very little of the estate has measured energy data. Mostly estimates or absent.
Measured data for the largest sites only. Smaller sites, edge and cloud rely on estimates.
Measured data for most major on-premise sites. Cloud energy estimated from provider tools or usage data.
Strong measured coverage across on-premise. Cloud energy derived from credible provider telemetry.
Comprehensive measured or provider-verified energy data across on-premise, colo, cloud and edge environments.

questions.text: Can you link energy use back to services, platforms, teams or business demand?

options.label: Cannot link energy to services, platforms, teams or business demand.
Only broad allocation — e.g. total IT energy divided by headcount or floor area.
Can attribute energy to major platforms or environments (e.g. per data hall, per cluster).
Energy attributable to services or teams for most of the on-premise estate. Cloud attribution via provider tools.
Service-level or workload-level energy attribution across on-premise and cloud. Validated allocation rules documented.

questions.text: How often is energy data available at a useful level?

options.label: Annual or less. Energy data only available for reporting exercises.
Quarterly or monthly site totals. Not frequent enough for operational use.
Monthly data for most sites. Some facilities have weekly or near-real-time smart meter feeds.
Weekly or better for main sites. Monthly for smaller environments. Sufficient for regular management use.
Near-real-time or daily for key sites. Continuous feeds integrated into dashboards and operational workflows.

questions.text: How quickly can you get the data after the energy has been used?

options.label: Significant lag — weeks or months before energy data is available.
Data typically 2-4 weeks behind. Depends on utility billing cycles.
Main sites within 1-2 weeks. Smart-metered sites faster. Some lag for provider-reported data.
Most data available within days. Smart meter and BMS data near-real-time for key sites.
Operational data available within hours or continuously. Billing data reconciled within days.

questions.text: Does the data reflect where the energy was actually used?

options.label: Cannot tell where energy was consumed — only total site or contract figures.
Broad site or provider level only. Cannot distinguish between halls, environments or IT functions.
Location clear for main on-premise facilities. Cloud region-level for major providers.
Hall or environment-level for on-premise. Cloud availability zone level. Location-specific for most of the estate.
Precise: rack row, zone, AZ-level attribution. Location accuracy supports real allocation and optimisation decisions.

questions.text: Can you show clearly where the energy data came from and how it was calculated?

options.label: Source and calculation method unclear or undocumented.
Main sources known (meters, invoices, provider reports) but calculation method not well documented.
Sources documented. Calculation approach (measured vs modelled vs estimated) identified for main data streams.
Clear lineage from source to output. Measured vs modelled clearly flagged. Confidence levels assigned.
Full data provenance with audit trail. Source, method, confidence and limitations all transparent and verifiable.

questions.text: How confident are you in the accuracy and calibration of your energy measurement infrastructure — meters, sensors, and data collection systems?
questions.guidance: Measured energy data is only as good as the measurement infrastructure. Sub-metering quality, calibration frequency, and data collection reliability directly affect the credibility of all downstream calculations.
questions.evidence_hint: Meter calibration records, sub-metering architecture documentation, data quality audit results

options.label: No visibility into meter quality or calibration status.
We know where meters are but calibration is irregular and data gaps are not tracked.
Major meters identified and periodically calibrated. Data gaps detected but remediation is slow.
Meter infrastructure documented and calibrated on schedule. Data gaps tracked and escalated.
Full measurement assurance: documented calibration, automated gap detection, sub-metering strategy and continuous quality monitoring.

domains.name: Water data
domains.definition: Assesses the quality, applicability and context of water inputs used in GreenOps calculations, particularly for data centre, cooling and AI-relevant workloads.
domains.why_it_matters: Water is often under-measured yet increasingly material. Weak water data limits reporting, site strategy, cooling assessment and environmental trade-off decisions.
recommendation_themes: Establish basic water reporting where material
Link water to cooling modes and site context
Bring in local water stress context

scoring.normalisation: weighted average of selected option scores (1-5), rounded to nearest whole number with .75 thresholds upward
question_override_rules: If more than 30% of answers are level 1, overall maturity cannot exceed 3 unless assessor override is applied.
If evidence coverage is weak across more than half of weighted questions, confidence should be reduced.
If assurance/lineage questions score 1 or 2, decision-readiness should be capped at directional support unless other evidence is strong and override is used.

confidence_logic.medium: Reasonable evidence coverage with some gaps or assumptions.

questions.text: Do you have water consumption data for the facilities or providers that matter to your GreenOps calculations?

options.label: No water consumption data available for any facility or provider.
Broad water totals from invoices or provider sustainability reports for largest sites only.
Site-level water data for main facilities. Provider-reported figures for colo. Some sites still missing.
Measured water data for most owned and operated facilities. Colo providers reporting per contract.
Comprehensive water data across owned, leased and colo environments. Metered where practical, provider-verified elsewhere.

questions.text: How detailed is the water data you receive or collect?

options.label: No detail — just total consumption figures if anything.
Total site consumption only. No split between cooling, process and domestic water use.
Cooling water separated from domestic for main sites. WUE calculable for key facilities.
Detailed: cooling tower, chiller loop, adiabatic and domestic water separately tracked for main sites.
Granular water data per cooling system, per hall or zone. Full water balance with losses, recycling and blowdown tracked.

questions.text: Can you link water use meaningfully to cooling or IT activity?

options.label: No link between water use and IT or cooling activity.
Very broad — total water divided by IT load to derive a rough WUE.
Can link water use to cooling systems at site level. WUE derived from measured data for main sites.
Water linked to specific cooling systems and correlated with IT load per hall or zone.
Water use attributable to cooling systems per zone or hall. Correlation with IT load, weather and cooling mode validated.

questions.text: How often is water data available at a useful level?

options.label: Annually or less. Water data only appears for sustainability reports.
Quarterly or when invoices arrive. Not operationally useful.
Monthly for main sites. Sufficient for trend reporting but not operational management.
Weekly or better for main facilities. Monthly for colo-provided data.
Daily or near-real-time for owned sites. Integrated into cooling management and capacity dashboards.

questions.text: Does the water data reflect where it is being used and the local context?

options.label: No awareness of where water is being consumed or the local water stress context.
Broad site-level only. No local water stress or scarcity context applied.
Site-level with some awareness of water stress classification for major locations.
Location-specific water data with water stress risk classification applied per site.
Detailed per-facility water data with local water stress, scarcity and regulatory context integrated into decision-making.

questions.text: How quickly can you access water data after the usage happens?

options.label: Months of lag — water data arrives with utility bills or annual reports.
Weeks of lag. Depends on invoicing or provider reporting cycles.
Main sites within 1-2 weeks. Colo data monthly or quarterly.
Most data within days for owned sites. Provider data within weeks.
Near-real-time for metered sites. Provider data within days via automated feeds.

questions.text: How strong is the method behind your water calculations?

options.label: No clear method. Numbers used without understanding how they were derived.
Relies on invoiced totals or provider estimates. No independent validation.
Metered data for main sites. Provider-reported data accepted without audit.
Metered data validated against invoices. Provider data checked against contractual commitments.
Full water accounting: metered, reconciled, audited. Provider data verified with documented confidence levels.

questions.text: Can you explain clearly where the water data came from and how much confidence you have in it?

options.label: Source of water data unclear. Cannot explain provenance if challenged.
Know the broad source (invoices, meters, provider reports) but detail and confidence not documented.
Sources documented for main sites. Confidence levels estimated but not formally tracked.
Clear provenance with confidence levels for most data streams. Known gaps documented.
Full data lineage with confidence scoring, source validation and audit trail for all water data.

domains.name: Infrastructure efficiency metric quality
domains.definition: Assesses the quality and applicability of PUE, WUE, CUE, CER, CLF and similar metrics used to translate IT activity into total infrastructure impact.
domains.why_it_matters: Weak efficiency metrics can distort non-IT load allocation and make outputs look more precise than they really are.
recommendation_themes: Replace design or marketing values with operational values

scoring.normalisation: weighted average of selected option scores (1-5), rounded to nearest whole number with .75 thresholds upward
question_override_rules: If more than 30% of answers are level 1, overall maturity cannot exceed 3 unless assessor override is applied.
If evidence coverage is weak across more than half of weighted questions, confidence should be reduced.
If assurance/lineage questions score 1 or 2, decision-readiness should be capped at directional support unless other evidence is strong and override is used.

confidence_logic.low: Sparse evidence, inconsistent notes, or material contradictions across answers.
confidence_logic.medium: Reasonable evidence coverage with some gaps or assumptions.
confidence_logic.high: Strong evidence coverage, consistent answers, clear ownership, documented methods.

questions.text: What is the main basis for metrics like PUE, WUE, CUE and similar measures used in your calculations?

options.label: No measured PUE, WUE or similar metrics. Using published averages or guesses.
PUE derived from broad energy totals (total facility energy / estimated IT load). Other metrics not calculated.
PUE calculated from metered IT and facility energy for main sites. WUE or CUE attempted for some.
PUE, WUE calculated from metered data across most sites. CLF or CUE where relevant. Methods align with ISO 30134.
Full suite of efficiency metrics (PUE, WUE, CUE, REF, CLF) from metered data. ISO 30134 compliant. Independently verifiable.

questions.text: Are the boundaries for these metrics clearly defined and used consistently?

options.label: No defined boundaries. Metrics calculated inconsistently or not at all.
Boundaries loosely defined. Inconsistent inclusion of UPS, cooling, lighting across sites.
Boundaries documented for main sites following EU CoC or ISO guidance. Some inconsistency remains.
Consistent boundary definitions applied across the portfolio. Aligned to ISO 30134 or equivalent.
Rigorous, documented boundaries per site. Independently auditable. Consistent across all reporting.

questions.text: Do the values reflect real operating conditions?

options.label: Metrics are nameplate, design or published values — not measured under real conditions.
Broadly reflect average conditions but not adjusted for load, season or operating mode.
Reflect real conditions for main sites under typical load. Seasonal variation acknowledged but not fully captured.
Measured under real operating conditions. Seasonal, load-dependent and partial-load effects captured.
Real-time or near-real-time metrics reflecting actual operating conditions. Partial-load, seasonal and mode effects all visible.

questions.text: Are these metrics detailed enough for where you are applying them?

options.label: Only portfolio-level or single-site averages. No detail below site level.
Site-level metrics for largest facilities. No hall, zone or system-level breakdown.
Site-level for most facilities. Some hall-level PUE for largest sites.
Hall or zone-level metrics for main facilities. System-level (cooling, UPS) efficiency visible where metered.
Zone, hall and system-level efficiency metrics. Granular enough to identify specific improvement opportunities.

questions.text: Do they reflect the actual site, facility, hall or environment they relate to?

options.label: Metrics are portfolio averages or generic values — not site-specific.
Site-specific for one or two main locations. Others use portfolio averages.
Site-specific for most owned or operated facilities. Colo uses provider-reported values.
Site-specific across the portfolio. Colo data received and validated per site.
Per-site, per-hall metrics consistently derived. Provider data verified. No portfolio averaging masks site performance.

questions.text: How often are these metrics refreshed or checked?

options.label: Calculated annually or only for sustainability reports.
Quarterly recalculation for main sites. Annual for others.
Monthly recalculation for main facilities. Quarterly for smaller or provider-reported sites.
Monthly or more frequent across the portfolio. Trend analysis active.
Continuous or near-real-time where metered. Automated alerts for efficiency degradation.

questions.text: How confident are you that these metrics support credible allocation of non-IT overhead?

options.label: Cannot use these metrics to allocate overhead costs or environmental impact credibly.
Metrics used for rough allocation (e.g. flat PUE multiplier) but not validated for accuracy.
Metrics support credible site-level allocation of cooling and infrastructure overhead to IT load.
Validated metrics used for overhead allocation at site and hall level. Method documented and defensible.
Metrics embedded in cost and carbon allocation models. Allocation rules auditable and aligned with reporting frameworks.

questions.text: Can you show where these metrics came from and how they were derived?

options.label: Cannot explain how metrics were derived. No documentation.
Broad method known but not documented. Would be difficult to reproduce or audit.
Method documented for main sites. Inputs, boundaries and calculation approach available.
Full method documentation with data sources, measurement points, boundary definitions and assumptions.
Auditable methodology with full provenance. Aligned to standards (ISO 30134, EU CoC). Independently verifiable.

domains.name: Embodied emissions data
domains.definition: Assesses the specificity, reliability and usefulness of embodied emissions inputs used for lifecycle, procurement, refresh and circularity decisions.
domains.why_it_matters: Embodied emissions are increasingly material. Weak inputs lead to poor refresh assumptions and weak supplier challenge.
recommendation_themes: Support reuse and refresh extension decisions

scoring.normalisation: weighted average of selected option scores (1-5), rounded to nearest whole number with .75 thresholds upward
question_override_rules: If more than 30% of answers are level 1, overall maturity cannot exceed 3 unless assessor override is applied.
If evidence coverage is weak across more than half of weighted questions, confidence should be reduced.
If assurance/lineage questions score 1 or 2, decision-readiness should be capped at directional support unless other evidence is strong and override is used.

confidence_logic.low: Sparse evidence, inconsistent notes, or material contradictions across answers.
confidence_logic.medium: Reasonable evidence coverage with some gaps or assumptions.
confidence_logic.high: Strong evidence coverage, consistent answers, clear ownership, documented methods.

questions.text: What do you mainly use today to estimate embodied emissions?

options.label: No process for estimating embodied emissions. Not attempted or purely guesswork.
Spend-based proxies using procurement value and generic spend-to-carbon conversion factors.
Category-average emission factors applied by asset class (e.g. kgCO2e per server, per laptop).
Vendor-specific LCAs or EPDs used for major product families, but not matched to actual configurations.
Product-configuration-specific LCAs or EPDs matched to actual assets deployed. Updated with each procurement cycle.

questions.text: How specific is that embodied data to the actual products and assets you use?

options.label: No product-specific data. Generic factors or no factors at all.
Spend-based factors only — no link to actual product type or specification.
Category averages by asset type (e.g. 'rack server', '14-inch laptop') but not product-specific.
Vendor-specific EPDs or LCAs for main product lines. Not matched to the exact model or configuration deployed.
Configuration-specific: matched to actual model, spec and options. Vendor EPDs linked to asset records.

questions.text: How much of your estate can you match to embodied emissions data with reasonable confidence?

options.label: Cannot match any assets to embodied emissions data.
Can apply spend-based proxies to procurement data. Covers broad totals but not asset-level.
Category factors applied to most servers, storage and networking. End-user devices partially covered.
Vendor or product-specific data for most asset classes. Coverage gaps limited to minor or legacy categories.
Comprehensive: >90% of assets matched to product-specific or configuration-specific embodied data.

questions.text: Do you link embodied data to real lifecycle context such as age, refresh plans, reuse and end of life?

options.label: No link between embodied data and asset lifecycle context.
Procurement date recorded. No link to refresh plans, reuse potential or end-of-life routing.
Age and basic refresh eligibility tracked. Embodied impact considered at aggregate level for refresh planning.
Embodied data linked to lifecycle status: age, warranty, refresh schedule, reuse potential. Informs refresh business cases.
Full lifecycle integration: embodied carbon amortised over useful life, refresh trade-offs quantified, circularity options costed.

questions.text: Can you reflect supplier-specific or product-specific differences where they matter?

options.label: No ability to reflect supplier or product differences in embodied data.
All products within a category treated the same. No supplier differentiation.
Some awareness of supplier differences (e.g. Dell vs HPE) but not systematically applied.
Vendor-specific embodied data used for major suppliers. Product-line differences reflected.
Supplier and product-specific differences actively used in procurement. Carbon intensity comparison between suppliers standard practice.

questions.text: Is the embodied dataset current enough to support procurement and refresh decisions?

options.label: Embodied data is static, outdated or nonexistent. Cannot support procurement decisions.
Data refreshed annually at most. Too stale for active procurement use.
Updated when new product ranges are introduced. Reasonably current for major categories.
Refreshed quarterly or with each procurement cycle. Current enough for refresh and procurement decisions.
Continuously maintained. New products added as they enter the catalogue. Embedded in procurement workflows.

questions.text: Can you explain where the embodied values came from and how strong the evidence is?

options.label: Cannot explain where embodied values come from. No documented sources.
Broad source known (e.g. 'Defra factors', 'vendor website') but detail and reliability not assessed.
Sources documented: which EPDs, LCA databases or factor sets are used. Some confidence assessment.
Clear provenance for all embodied data. Source, methodology, age and confidence level documented per factor.
Full evidence chain: EPD/LCA source, publication date, scope, methodology, validation status. Audit-ready.

questions.text: Is the embodied data good enough to support real decisions, or only broad reporting?

options.label: Data is not usable for decisions. Only broad reporting at best.
Supports rough portfolio-level estimates for sustainability reports. Not decision-grade.
Supports comparison between product categories and informs refresh timing at aggregate level.
Supports procurement decisions: vendor comparison, refresh vs extend analysis, circularity trade-offs.
Decision-grade: embedded in procurement governance, supplier scorecards and lifecycle investment cases.

questions.text: For major hardware suppliers, do you have visibility into their manufacturing carbon intensity? Is this factored into supplier selection?
questions.guidance: Supplier carbon intensity varies dramatically. Many major vendors now publish Scope 1+2 emissions data. Using this in procurement decisions is a material lever for reducing embodied carbon.
questions.evidence_hint: Supplier environmental disclosures, procurement evaluation criteria, supplier scorecards

options.label: No visibility into supplier manufacturing carbon intensity.
Some suppliers publish sustainability reports but we do not extract or use manufacturing-specific data.
Manufacturing carbon data available for major suppliers. Reviewed but not formally used in selection.
Supplier carbon intensity is a formal procurement criterion. Compared across shortlisted vendors.
Supplier manufacturing carbon data integrated into embodied calculations. Trade-offs between cost and carbon explicit in procurement.

questions.text: In refresh and procurement cycles, do you systematically evaluate circular options such as refurbished, remanufactured, or extended-life alternatives?
questions.guidance: Circular alternatives can dramatically reduce embodied carbon. This requires explicit evaluation and quantified trade-off analysis rather than defaulting to new hardware procurement.
questions.evidence_hint: Procurement policies on refurbished hardware, embodied carbon comparisons, circular inventory tracking

options.label: No consideration of circular options. Always procure new.
Refurbished considered only if vendor-proposed and significantly cheaper. No embodied carbon comparison.
Circular options (refurbished, extended-life) evaluated for some asset classes. Embodied savings estimated.
Refurbished vs new is standard in procurement evaluation. Embodied carbon trade-offs quantified.
Circular is the default consideration. Refurbished, extended-life and resale options actively managed with embodied carbon comparison as standard.

domains.name: Carbon intensity and emissions factors
domains.definition: Assesses the quality, precision and governance of emissions factors used to convert activity data into carbon outputs.
domains.why_it_matters: Poor emissions factors can distort otherwise good energy or usage data and create weak location- or time-based decisions.
recommendation_themes: Increase temporal and locational precision where justified

questions.text: What is the main basis for the emissions factors you use today?

options.label: No emissions factors applied. Carbon not calculated or based on rough guesses.
Generic national or regional grid average factors (e.g. single UK grid factor for all electricity).
Location-based factors using published grid intensity data (e.g. DEFRA, IEA, AIB residual mix).
Location-based and market-based factors applied correctly. Supplier tariff and RE procurement reflected.
Full factor hierarchy: location-based, market-based, supplier-specific, time-of-use. REGOs, PPAs and green tariffs handled correctly.

questions.text: Do the factors reflect the actual location that matters for the decision?

options.label: Single factor applied regardless of location or supplier.
Country-level factors applied. No regional, grid-zone or provider-level differentiation.
Regional or grid-zone factors for main locations. Provider-specific where contractual data available.
Location-specific factors at grid zone or site level. Provider and tariff differences reflected.
Precise: site-specific factors reflecting local grid, supplier tariff, RE procurement and time-of-use where available.

questions.text: How time-sensitive are the factors you use?

options.label: Using factors from unknown or outdated sources. Possibly years old.
Annual factors applied. Same factor used for all periods within a year.
Annual factors updated yearly from credible sources. Monthly granularity for some locations.
Monthly or quarterly factors for main locations. Annual factors refreshed promptly when published.
Time-of-use or half-hourly marginal factors where available. Annual and monthly factors updated automatically.

questions.text: Are location-based, market-based and supplier-specific methods clearly separated and governed?

options.label: No distinction between location-based and market-based accounting.
Aware of the distinction but only one method applied (usually location-based).
Both methods calculated for main scope. Market-based includes green tariff claims but not fully validated.
Clear separation with documented methodology. Market-based reflects REGOs, PPAs and supplier contracts.
Dual reporting standard. Market-based fully validated against contractual evidence. Additionality assessed where claimed.

questions.text: Do you have fit-for-purpose factor logic across the estate, or do you rely on broad defaults?

options.label: One factor applied everywhere regardless of location, provider or energy source.
Broad factors by country or region. No differentiation by provider, tariff or contract.
Different factors for different locations and main providers. Some estate still on generic defaults.
Fit-for-purpose factors across most of the estate. Provider, tariff and location differences reflected.
Granular factor logic across the full estate. Automated factor selection based on site, provider, tariff and time period.

questions.text: How quickly are factor changes picked up in your calculations?

options.label: Factors rarely updated. May be using data from several years ago.
Updated annually when someone remembers or when reports are due.
Updated annually from published sources. Process documented but manual.
Updated within weeks of new data publication. Process owned and scheduled.
Automated updates from authoritative sources. Factor changes flagged and validated before application.

questions.text: Can you explain where the factors came from and why you chose them?

options.label: Cannot explain which factors are used or where they came from.
Broad source known (e.g. 'DEFRA', 'IEA') but specific factor selection rationale not documented.
Factor sources documented. Selection rationale recorded for main scope areas.
Full factor provenance: source, version, publication date, applicability and selection rationale documented.
Audit-ready: factor register with provenance, methodology notes, update history and rationale. Aligned to reporting standards.

questions.text: Are the factors good enough for real GreenOps decisions, or just broad reporting?

options.label: Factors are too crude for any meaningful GreenOps decision beyond rough estimates.
Support broad portfolio-level carbon reporting. Not reliable enough for site or service-level decisions.
Support credible site-level footprint reporting and high-level procurement comparisons.
Support operational decisions: site comparison, supplier evaluation, RE procurement impact assessment.
Decision-grade: support investment cases, migration planning, supplier scorecards and governance reporting.

questions.text: Can you distinguish between location-based and market-based carbon accounting? Do you track renewable energy procurement, PPAs, and REC sourcing with appropriate governance?
questions.guidance: Market-based carbon accounting relies on quality of REC sourcing and PPA governance. Location-based versus market-based distinction is essential for credible reporting. Additionality and temporal matching are key quality indicators.
questions.evidence_hint: REC certificates, PPA documentation, renewable energy tracking, carbon accounting methodology documentation

options.label: No ability to distinguish between location-based and market-based accounting.
Aware of the difference but do not track or report separately.
Both methods applied for main scope. Market-based reflects tariff claims but limited contractual evidence.
Dual reporting with documented RE instruments (REGOs, GoOs, PPAs). Procurement impact clearly shown.
Full RE accounting: instruments validated, additionality assessed, temporal matching applied where feasible. Supports RE procurement strategy.

domains.name: Asset, workload, application and service usage maturity
domains.definition: Assesses whether the organisation understands whether infrastructure and services are being used efficiently and meaningfully, including VM-, app-, service- and AI-relevant usage signals.
domains.why_it_matters: Without utilisation and service usage visibility, GreenOps cannot move from reporting to avoiding low-value demand and optimising live services.
recommendation_themes: Link usage to services and owners
Include AI prompts/tokens/inference metrics where relevant

questions.text: How much visibility do you have into how infrastructure is actually being used?

options.label: No visibility into infrastructure utilisation. Provisioned capacity assumed to be used.
Periodic manual checks on major platforms. Utilisation sampled rather than continuously measured.
Monitoring tools deployed on main compute platforms. CPU, memory, storage utilisation visible for key environments.
Comprehensive utilisation monitoring across compute, storage and networking for most environments.
Real-time utilisation telemetry across the estate. Integrated into capacity management, rightsizing and efficiency workflows.

questions.text: How well do you measure real application or service usage?

options.label: No measurement of application or service-level usage.
Basic availability monitoring (up/down) but no usage or consumption metrics.
Transaction volumes, active users or request rates tracked for major applications.
Service-level usage metrics (transactions, sessions, API calls, data volumes) for most business-facing services.
Comprehensive service usage telemetry linked to business outcomes. Demand patterns analysed and forecast.

questions.text: Can you link usage back to a named service, owner, product, user group or business purpose?

options.label: Cannot link resource usage to any named service, team or business owner.
Some major platforms or applications identifiable but most usage is unattributed.
Usage attributable to named services or teams for main compute environments. Storage and networking less clear.
Strong service-level attribution across most platforms. Cloud tagging enforced. Ownership and cost centre linked.
Full attribution: every workload, service and resource linked to owner, cost centre and business function. Validated continuously.

questions.text: How well do you spot idle, oversized, dormant or low-value consumption?

options.label: No ability to identify idle, oversized or dormant resources.
Occasional manual review identifies obvious waste. No systematic process.
Rightsizing recommendations generated for major cloud and virtualised platforms. On-premise waste less visible.
Systematic identification of idle, oversized and dormant resources across cloud and on-premise. Remediation tracked.
Automated detection with remediation workflows. Rightsizing, decommission and consolidation actions tracked to completion.

questions.text: How often can you see changes in usage patterns?

options.label: No regular view of usage patterns. Snapshots only if someone investigates.
Monthly or quarterly utilisation reports for major platforms.
Weekly or bi-weekly utilisation data for main environments. Trends visible over months.
Daily or real-time utilisation data for most environments. Pattern changes detected promptly.
Continuous telemetry with anomaly detection. Usage pattern changes trigger automated alerts and actions.

questions.text: Can you compare usage properly across environments, regions or hosting types?

options.label: Cannot compare usage across different environments, regions or hosting types.
Can compare at a very broad level (e.g. total cloud vs on-premise) but not like-for-like.
Comparable utilisation metrics for similar platform types. Cross-environment comparison possible for major workloads.
Normalised utilisation metrics across environments. Migration and placement decisions supported by usage data.
Full cross-estate comparability. Workload efficiency compared across cloud, on-premise and colo with normalised metrics.

questions.text: If you run AI services, how well do you measure things like prompts, tokens, inference calls, training jobs and model use?

options.label: No measurement of AI/ML resource consumption.
Aware of AI workloads but no specific usage telemetry (tokens, GPU hours, inference volumes).
Basic GPU utilisation and job run-time tracked for main AI/ML platforms.
Detailed: GPU utilisation, training vs inference split, token volumes, model serving costs tracked for most AI workloads.
Comprehensive AI observability: GPU efficiency, token economics, training cost per model, inference per request. Integrated into FinOps.

questions.text: Can you explain where the usage data came from and how much confidence you have in it?

options.label: Cannot explain where usage data comes from or how reliable it is.
Main tools known (e.g. vCenter, CloudWatch) but data quality and coverage not assessed.
Sources documented. Coverage and confidence understood for main platforms.
Clear data lineage from monitoring tools through to reporting. Confidence levels assigned per data stream.
Full provenance with automated quality checks. Data gaps detected and flagged. Source reliability continuously validated.

questions.text: Do you track and govern resource consumption for AI/ML workloads including training jobs, fine-tuning, and batch inference?
questions.guidance: Training jobs and fine-tuning consume significant GPU resources. Governance means tracking who initiates jobs, what they use, expected business value, and cost/carbon approval gates.
questions.evidence_hint: ML training job logs, approval system records, cost allocation for GPU infrastructure

options.label: No governance of AI/ML resource consumption.
AI workloads run without resource budgets or consumption tracking.
Training and inference resource consumption tracked for major AI projects. No formal governance.
AI resource governance in place: budgets, approvals, consumption tracking for training and inference.
Full AI resource governance: training budgets, inference cost controls, model efficiency targets. Embedded in ML platform policies.

questions.text: For cloud-hosted AI services such as LLM APIs, can you track usage volumes and associated carbon impact?
questions.guidance: Many organisations use cloud AI APIs (GPT-4, Claude, etc). Usage is measurable via tokens and inference calls. Major cloud providers and AI vendors increasingly publish carbon intensity data.
questions.evidence_hint: Cloud AI service usage logs, API call tracking, cost allocation by service, carbon intensity estimates

options.label: No visibility into cloud-hosted AI service usage or costs.
Total spend on AI API services known at account level. No breakdown by model, use case or team.
Usage volumes (API calls, tokens) tracked for major AI services. Cost allocated to projects.
Detailed tracking: tokens, model choice, latency, cost per request. Attributed to teams and use cases.
Full AI service observability: usage, cost, performance and carbon per request. Optimisation recommendations active.

questions.text: Do you systematically optimise AI workloads for computational efficiency — for example through prompt engineering, model quantisation, inference caching, or batch processing?
questions.guidance: AI workloads can be 10x more efficient with targeted optimisation. Prompt engineering (shorter context), model quantisation (faster/cheaper), inference caching (avoid re-inference), and batch processing all reduce resource consumption.
questions.evidence_hint: Prompt design guidelines, model selection criteria, quantisation policies, inference cost tracking

options.label: No optimisation of AI workloads for computational efficiency.
Aware of efficiency differences (e.g. model size, quantisation) but no systematic action.
Some optimisation: model selection considers efficiency. Batch vs real-time inference considered.
Systematic optimisation: model distillation, quantisation, efficient architectures, scheduling. Tracked against targets.
Continuous AI efficiency programme: workload scheduling, hardware matching, model optimisation, carbon-aware inference. Embedded in ML platform.

Link impacts to owners and services

questions.text: How detailed is your current attribution of environmental impact?

options.label: No attribution. Environmental impact reported at total estate or site level only.
Broad allocation by headcount, floor space or equal split. Not reflective of actual consumption.
IT load-based allocation for main data centres. Cloud cost allocation used as a proxy for cloud emissions.
Measured IT load attribution to services or teams for most of the on-premise estate. Cloud tagged and attributed.
Workload-level attribution across on-premise and cloud. Measured, validated and granular enough for service-level accountability.

questions.text: How credible is the method you use to attribute impact?

options.label: No attribution method in place.
Simple equal-split or headcount-based allocation. Not reflective of actual resource consumption.
Proportional allocation based on IT load (e.g. kW per rack, VM count). Documented but with known limitations.
Measured attribution using actual resource consumption data. Allocation rules documented and reviewed.
Validated, auditable attribution methodology. Rules peer-reviewed, aligned with FinOps/TBM and independently defensible.

questions.text: How much of the footprint can you attribute meaningfully rather than leaving at portfolio level?

options.label: All impact sits at estate or site level. Nothing meaningfully attributed.
Major platforms or data halls attributed. Most impact still at aggregate level.
Most on-premise IT load attributed to teams or services. Cloud emissions attributed via spend or usage.
Strong coverage: >75% of footprint attributed to named services or owners.
Comprehensive: >90% attributed at service or workload level. Unattributed residual tracked and managed.

questions.text: Can you link attributed impact back to named owners, products or teams?

options.label: No link between attributed impact and accountable owners.
Some major platforms linked to a team or cost centre. Most attribution stops at infrastructure level.
Most attributed impact linked to a named service or team. Business owner identified for major areas.
Clear ownership chain: impact attributed to service, team, cost centre and business owner for most scope.
Full accountability: every attributed unit of impact has a named owner. Linked to performance management and governance.

questions.text: Does the attribution reflect actual usage and demand, or mostly static allocation rules?

options.label: No measurement of actual usage. Any allocation is static.
Mostly static allocation (e.g. provisioned capacity, fixed shares). Does not reflect actual demand.
Mix of measured and static. Main compute platforms use actual load; storage and networking often static.
Mostly measured. Attribution reflects actual resource consumption for compute, storage and major cloud services.
Fully dynamic. Attribution updates with actual usage. Reflects demand patterns, scaling and workload migration.

questions.text: Where location changes impact materially, does your attribution reflect that?

options.label: Location not considered in attribution.
Same attribution rules applied regardless of location. No adjustment for local carbon intensity or efficiency.
Location factored in for major sites (e.g. different PUE applied per site).
Site-specific PUE, WUE and carbon factors applied. Location-sensitive attribution for most scope.
Full location-aware attribution: site, zone, provider and grid-specific factors applied automatically.

questions.text: Can you explain the attribution method clearly to stakeholders?

options.label: Cannot explain how attribution works. No documentation.
Broad approach understood by a few people but not documented or explained to stakeholders.
Method documented and can be explained at a high level. Limitations acknowledged.
Clear methodology documentation shared with stakeholders. Assumptions and limitations transparent.
Auditable methodology with stakeholder-facing documentation. Regularly reviewed and updated with governance oversight.

questions.text: Is the attribution good enough to drive real decisions and accountability?

options.label: Attribution is not trusted or used for decisions.
Used for broad sustainability reporting only. Not trusted for operational or financial decisions.
Supports directional decisions: identifying high-impact areas, prioritising improvement efforts.
Trusted enough for chargeback, showback and investment prioritisation. Used in business cases.
Decision-grade: embedded in financial governance, procurement, capacity planning and performance management.

questions.text: Are your carbon attribution and cost allocation methods aligned — using the same cost drivers and allocation keys?
questions.guidance: Carbon and cost should be allocated together using the same drivers (CPU hours, storage GB, etc). Misalignment creates confusion and blocks operational action. This alignment is essential for FinOps-GreenOps integration.
questions.evidence_hint: Cost allocation model documentation, carbon allocation model, FinOps and GreenOps governance alignment

options.label: Carbon attribution and cost allocation are completely separate with no alignment.
Aware that alignment would be valuable but methods, boundaries and data sources differ.
Some alignment for major areas (e.g. cloud spend maps roughly to cloud carbon). Not systematic.
Aligned for most scope: same boundaries, similar allocation keys, consistent ownership mapping.
Fully integrated: carbon and cost use the same allocation model. FinOps and GreenOps operate from a shared data foundation.

questions.text: How much of your cloud estate has usable telemetry for GreenOps analysis?

Usage and cost data extracted from cloud provider consoles or APIs for main accounts.
Structured telemetry pipeline covering most cloud accounts. FinOps tooling integrated.
Comprehensive multi-cloud observability: cost, usage, carbon and efficiency data across all accounts and providers.

questions.text: How detailed is the cloud telemetry you can access?

options.label: No meaningful detail. Only total cloud spend or high-level account summaries.
Account or subscription-level cost and basic usage data. No resource-level visibility.
Resource-level cost and usage for main services (compute, storage, database). Some tagging gaps.
Detailed resource-level telemetry with service-specific metrics. Tag coverage enforced for most accounts.
Granular: per-resource, per-hour cost and usage. Carbon intensity per service. Comprehensive tagging with governance.

questions.text: Can you link cloud telemetry back to tags, owners, products, services or business units?

options.label: Cannot link cloud telemetry to business owners, products or services.
Cloud accounts or subscriptions loosely mapped to teams. No resource-level attribution.
Tag-based attribution for main accounts. Most compute and storage attributed. Some untagged spend remains.
Strong tagging governance. Most cloud spend and usage attributed to services, teams and cost centres.
Full attribution: every cloud resource tagged and attributed. Untagged spend governed and remediated. Linked to business services.

questions.text: How often is cloud telemetry available at a useful level?

options.label: Cloud data reviewed annually or only for budget exercises.
Monthly cost reports reviewed. No real-time or weekly telemetry available.
Weekly or daily cost and usage data available for main accounts. Monthly trend analysis.
Daily telemetry for most accounts. Real-time alerts for anomalies and budget thresholds.
Continuous real-time telemetry. Integrated into operational dashboards with automated anomaly detection.

questions.text: Does the telemetry reflect the actual cloud region, zone or environment where it matters?

options.label: Cloud region or location not tracked in telemetry.
Region known at account level. No zone or environment-level detail.
Region and service-level location tracked for main workloads. Availability zone where relevant.
Detailed: region, availability zone and environment tracked. Location-specific carbon factors applicable.
Full location awareness: region, zone, environment. Carbon-aware placement recommendations based on real-time grid data.

questions.text: How strong is the method you use to turn telemetry into environmental insight?

options.label: No method for converting cloud telemetry into environmental insight.
Basic: total cloud spend multiplied by a generic emission factor.
Provider carbon dashboards used (e.g. AWS Customer Carbon Footprint, Azure Emissions Impact). Accepted at face value.
Provider data supplemented with independent calculations. Methodology documented. Limitations understood.
Robust multi-source method: provider data validated, supplemented with independent modelling and usage-based calculations.

questions.text: How quickly can you access cloud telemetry after the activity happens?

options.label: Cloud data significantly lagged — weeks or months behind.
Monthly billing data available. Usage data lagged by days to weeks.
Daily data available for main accounts. Some lag on carbon-specific data from providers.
Near-real-time cost and usage. Provider carbon data within days or weeks of activity.
Real-time cost and usage telemetry. Carbon data available within hours where provider APIs support it.

questions.text: Can you explain where the telemetry came from and how much confidence you have in it?

options.label: Cannot explain where cloud data comes from or how reliable it is.
Data comes from cloud console or billing. Reliability and completeness not assessed.
Sources documented (APIs, billing exports, FinOps tools). Known gaps and limitations recorded.
Clear data lineage from provider APIs through to reporting. Confidence levels assigned per data stream.
Full provenance: API sources, extraction methods, transformation logic and confidence all documented and auditable.

questions.text: Do you use cloud provider carbon intensity data to inform workload placement decisions — choosing lower-carbon regions or availability zones where practical?
questions.guidance: Major cloud providers publish carbon intensity by region. Using this data in workload placement decisions is a practical lever for reducing operational carbon, particularly for flexible workloads.
questions.evidence_hint: Cloud provider sustainability dashboards, region selection criteria, workload placement policies

options.label: Not aware of cloud provider carbon intensity data or its relevance to workload placement.
Aware that providers publish carbon data but not using it operationally.
Provider carbon data reviewed when making major migration or placement decisions.
Carbon intensity factored into workload placement. Low-carbon regions preferred where operationally feasible.
Carbon-aware scheduling and placement standard practice. Real-time grid carbon data used to optimise workload timing and location.

1.label: Initial / Ad hoc
1.description: Inputs are fragmented, reactive, estimate-heavy and not reliable enough for confident decision-making.

2.label: Managed / Repeatable
2.description: Inputs can be gathered and reused in a repeatable way, but coverage, precision and attribution remain inconsistent.

3.label: Defined / Standardised
3.description: Data sources, methods and ownership are documented and standardised across key areas.

4.label: Quantitatively Managed / Measured
4.description: Data quality, granularity, coverage and confidence are measured and controlled using evidence.

5.label: Optimising
5.description: Inputs are continuously improved, increasingly automated and embedded into operational and strategic decision-making.

supports: Broad awareness and high-level reporting for cloud service telemetry and workload data

does_not_support: Confident automation or tight target accountability

supports: Some controlled decisions in priority areas
does_not_support: Strong claims of precision across the whole estate

supports: Strong management, engineering and governance use
does_not_support: None by design; caveats should still be made explicit where relevant

recommendation_triggers.guidance: Prioritise foundational improvement in cloud service telemetry and workload data before making strong decision-grade claims.
Standardise and expand cloud service telemetry and workload data so it supports more controlled decisions.
Use stronger cloud service telemetry and workload data capability to drive optimisation and measurable intervention, not just reporting.

domains.name: Colocation and third-party provider data quality
domains.definition: Assesses the quality, transparency and usability of sustainability and operational data provided by colocation and other third-party providers.
domains.why_it_matters: Third-party environments are often a major blind spot. Weak provider data limits comparison, attribution, assurance and client confidence.
recommendation_themes: Link data requirements to contracts and reviews

scoring.normalisation: weighted average of selected option scores (1-5), rounded to nearest whole number with .75 thresholds upward
question_override_rules: If more than 30% of answers are level 1, overall maturity cannot exceed 3 unless assessor override is applied.
If evidence coverage is weak across more than half of weighted questions, confidence should be reduced.
If assurance/lineage questions score 1 or 2, decision-readiness should be capped at directional support unless other evidence is strong and override is used.

confidence_logic.low: Sparse evidence, inconsistent notes, or material contradictions across answers.
confidence_logic.medium: Reasonable evidence coverage with some gaps or assumptions.
confidence_logic.high: Strong evidence coverage, consistent answers, clear ownership, documented methods.

questions.text: How much useful sustainability and operational data do your providers actually give you?

options.label: Providers give no useful sustainability or operational data.
Annual sustainability reports or high-level PUE claims. Nothing site or customer-specific.
Some providers share site-level PUE and energy source data. Quality and format vary widely.
Main providers share customer-specific energy, PUE and RE data per site. Contractual reporting obligations in place.
Comprehensive: all providers share customer-specific energy, water, carbon, PUE and RE data. Verified and contractually required.

questions.text: How detailed is that provider data?

options.label: No detail beyond marketing claims or published averages.
Site-level PUE and total energy reported. No customer-specific or hall-level detail.
Customer-allocated energy and site PUE for main providers. Some provider-level carbon factors.
Per-cage or per-suite energy data from main providers. PUE, WUE and RE% per site. Carbon factors documented.
Granular: per-cage or per-kW metered energy, site-specific PUE, WUE, RE%, carbon factors with full methodology disclosure.

questions.text: Can you link provider data back to your own racks, cages, services or consumers?

options.label: Cannot link provider data to our own racks, services or consumers.
Provider data received at account level. Manual effort needed to map to our infrastructure.
Can map provider energy data to our cages or suites. Attribution to services requires manual work.
Provider data linked to our racks or cages. Attribution to internal services and teams possible for most scope.
Full traceability: provider data mapped to our racks, through to services, teams and business owners automatically.

questions.text: Does the provider data reflect the actual site or environment that matters?

options.label: Provider data is generic or portfolio-averaged. Not site-specific.
Data labelled as site-specific but may use portfolio averages or design-day values.
Site-specific metrics for main provider locations. Some smaller sites still on provider averages.
Site-specific, operationally measured data for most colo locations. Design vs operating values distinguished.
All provider data site-specific and operationally measured. Independently verifiable against contractual SLAs.

questions.text: How timely is the data from providers?

options.label: Provider data received annually at best, often only in sustainability reports.
Quarterly reporting from main providers. Annual from others.
Monthly data from main providers. Quarterly from smaller ones. Sufficient for reporting.
Monthly from most providers. Main providers offer near-real-time portal access.
Near-real-time or daily data via API or portal. Automated ingestion into our GreenOps data pipeline.

questions.text: How strong is the method behind the provider metrics and disclosures?

options.label: No understanding of how provider metrics were calculated.
Provider publishes PUE but method, boundary and measurement approach unclear.
Main providers document methodology. PUE boundary and measurement approach understood.
Provider methodology assessed and compared to standards (ISO 30134, EU CoC). Gaps and limitations documented.
Provider methodology independently validated. Measurement, boundary and methodology aligned with our standards. Audit rights exercised.

questions.text: Can the provider explain the source and evidence behind the numbers?

options.label: Provider cannot or will not explain the source and evidence behind reported numbers.
Provider references industry averages or design values. Actual measurement evidence not available.
Main providers can explain sources. Metered vs modelled distinguished for key metrics.
Providers share measurement methodology, data sources and confidence assessment. Evidence available on request.
Full transparency: providers share raw data, methodology, calibration records and audit trails. Contractually required.

questions.text: Is the provider data good enough to support decisions, or only high-level reporting?

options.label: Provider data not usable for any GreenOps decisions.
Supports broad portfolio-level reporting only. Not reliable for site or service-level decisions.
Supports site-level footprint reporting and high-level provider comparison.
Supports operational decisions: provider comparison, contract negotiation, allocation to services.
Decision-grade: supports provider selection, SLA enforcement, customer allocation and regulatory reporting.

domains.name: Temporal granularity and timeliness
domains.definition: Assesses whether data is available at the right time resolution and with low enough latency to support the decisions the organisation wants to make.
domains.why_it_matters: Even good data can be operationally weak if it is too old or too coarse in time.
recommendation_themes: Increase time resolution where it changes decisions
Align cadence to decision use cases

question_override_rules: If more than 30% of answers are level 1, overall maturity cannot exceed 3 unless assessor override is applied.

If assurance/lineage questions score 1 or 2, decision-readiness should be capped at directional support unless other evidence is strong and override is used.

confidence_logic.low: Sparse evidence, inconsistent notes, or material contradictions across answers.
confidence_logic.medium: Reasonable evidence coverage with some gaps or assumptions.
confidence_logic.high: Strong evidence coverage, consistent answers, clear ownership, documented methods.

questions.text: Across the main GreenOps inputs, how detailed is the time-based view you actually have?

options.label: Annual snapshots or point-in-time data only. No time-series view.
Quarterly aggregates for most domains. Monthly for one or two areas.
Monthly data for most domains. Weekly for some energy or utilisation metrics.
Weekly or better for most operational domains. Monthly for slower-moving areas like embodied data.
Near-real-time or daily for operational domains. Appropriate cadence matched to each domain's decision cycle.

questions.text: How quickly is data usually available after the underlying activity happens?

options.label: Weeks to months of lag. Data arrives with billing cycles or annual reports.
2-4 weeks lag for most domains. Depends on manual collection or billing cycles.
1-2 weeks for main domains. Some operational data (energy, utilisation) faster.
Days for most domains. Operational data within hours. Billing data within a week.
Hours or near-real-time for operational domains. All data within days of underlying activity.

questions.text: Are refresh cycles consistent and understood across the main domains?

options.label: No defined refresh cycles. Data updated when someone asks for it.
Some domains have scheduled updates but cycles vary and are not documented.
Refresh cycles documented for main domains. Most data updated on a predictable schedule.
Consistent, documented refresh cycles across all domains. Schedule owned and monitored.
Automated refresh with monitoring. SLAs defined for data freshness. Deviations flagged and escalated.

questions.text: Do you clearly identify when data is stale or no longer reliable?

options.label: No awareness of data staleness. Old data used without flagging.
Staleness sometimes noticed manually but no systematic detection.
Age of data visible in reporting. Stale data flagged for major domains.
Automated staleness detection for most domains. Stale data flagged in dashboards and reports.
Real-time freshness monitoring with automated alerts. Stale data quarantined or flagged before it reaches decisions.

questions.text: Is the time resolution good enough for the decisions you actually want to make?

options.label: Time resolution too coarse for any operational decision. Annual or less.
Sufficient for annual reporting but not for operational management.
Monthly resolution supports trend analysis and quarterly planning.
Weekly or better supports operational management and improvement tracking.
Time resolution matched to decision cadence: real-time for operations, monthly for planning, quarterly for governance.

questions.text: How much of the estate benefits from time resolution that is genuinely useful?

options.label: Very little of the estate benefits from useful time resolution.
Main data centre sites have reasonable timeliness. Rest of estate lagging.
Most on-premise and major cloud environments have useful time resolution.
Strong coverage across on-premise, cloud and colo. Only minor gaps.
Comprehensive: all environments have time resolution appropriate to their decision-support role.

questions.text: Where location or environment changes the timing logic, do you handle that properly?

options.label: Location and environment differences not reflected in timing logic.
Same timing assumptions applied everywhere regardless of location or context.
Some adjustment for different environments (e.g. cloud data faster than colo provider data).
Timing logic reflects operational reality per environment type. Provider data handled on its own cycle.
Fully adaptive: timing logic accounts for location, provider, data source and regulatory reporting requirements.

questions.text: Can you explain the timing characteristics of the data clearly to users?

options.label: Users have no idea how old the data is or when it was last updated.
Some awareness but not consistently communicated. Users sometimes ask and get inconsistent answers.
Data age and refresh dates visible in main reports and dashboards.
Timing metadata (collection date, refresh date, next update) available for all major data streams.
Full timing transparency: users see data age, refresh cycle, confidence and any staleness warnings inline.

domains.name: Data lineage, transparency and assurance
domains.definition: Assesses whether GreenOps data inputs, assumptions, transformations and outputs can be traced, explained and trusted.
domains.why_it_matters: Without lineage and assurance, the numbers may look sophisticated but remain hard to defend or govern.

question_override_rules: If more than 30% of answers are level 1, overall maturity cannot exceed 3 unless assessor override is applied.

If assurance/lineage questions score 1 or 2, decision-readiness should be capped at directional support unless other evidence is strong and override is used.

confidence_logic.medium: Reasonable evidence coverage with some gaps or assumptions.

questions.text: Can you clearly identify the source systems and datasets behind your outputs?

options.label: Cannot identify source systems or datasets behind GreenOps outputs.
Main sources known (e.g. 'comes from the CMDB' or 'from the energy provider') but not formally documented.
Source systems documented for main data domains. Data flow understood at a high level.
Clear source identification across all domains. Data flow diagrams and integration points documented.
Full source registry with automated lineage tracking. Every output traceable to source system, dataset and extraction method.

questions.text: Are the calculations, assumptions and transformations documented clearly enough for others to follow?

options.label: No documentation of calculations, assumptions or transformations.
Key people know the method but it is not written down. Knowledge is in heads not documents.
Main calculations documented. Key assumptions recorded. Some transformation logic still undocumented.
Comprehensive documentation of methodology, assumptions, transformations and allocation rules.
Fully documented and version-controlled. Calculation logic independently reviewable. Assumption register maintained.

questions.text: Could someone else reproduce the output if they needed to?

options.label: No. Outputs depend entirely on specific individuals. Not reproducible.
Partially — with significant effort and tacit knowledge from the original author.
Mostly reproducible for main outputs. Some secondary calculations depend on individual knowledge.
Reproducible by any competent analyst. Methods documented, data sources accessible, tools available.
Fully reproducible and automated. Any competent person can regenerate outputs from documented sources and methods.

questions.text: Do you track data quality issues, exceptions and estimation boundaries properly?

options.label: Data quality issues not tracked. Exceptions and estimation boundaries unknown.
Major issues known informally. No systematic tracking of exceptions or data quality.
Quality issues logged for main data streams. Estimation boundaries identified for key areas.
Systematic quality tracking: exceptions logged, estimation boundaries documented, remediation tracked.
Automated quality monitoring with exception management. Data quality KPIs tracked and reported to governance.

questions.text: Do users understand how much confidence to place in the outputs?

options.label: Users have no understanding of data confidence or limitations.
Some users aware of broad limitations but confidence not formally communicated.
Confidence levels communicated for main outputs. Caveats included in reporting.
Confidence scoring applied to outputs. Users understand where data is measured vs estimated vs modelled.
Transparent confidence framework: every output carries a confidence rating with explanation. Users trained on interpretation.

questions.text: Do you control and govern methodology changes properly?

options.label: No change control for GreenOps methodology.
Changes happen ad-hoc. Sometimes recognised retrospectively when numbers shift unexpectedly.
Major methodology changes documented and communicated. Minor changes less well controlled.
Formal change control for methodology. Impact assessed, stakeholders notified, historical comparisons maintained.
Full governance: change control board, impact assessment, version control, restatement policy and stakeholder communication.

questions.text: Is there a clear distinction between measured, modelled and estimated values?

options.label: No distinction between measured, modelled and estimated values.
Some awareness but not systematically flagged in data or reporting.
Main outputs flagged as measured, modelled or estimated. Users can see the broad basis.
All data streams categorised. Measured vs modelled vs estimated clearly shown in all reporting.
Full data quality taxonomy: measured, modelled, estimated, proxy. Each with documented confidence and methodology.

questions.text: Is the overall assurance level strong enough for the stakeholders using the outputs?

options.label: Assurance level insufficient for any external or governance use.
Sufficient for internal awareness but would not withstand external scrutiny.
Sufficient for internal reporting and management information. Some gaps for external assurance.
Sufficient for regulatory reporting and external disclosure. Methodology defensible under review.
Audit-ready: sufficient for independent third-party assurance. Methodology, data and controls all documented.

domains.name: Decision integration and optimisation workflow
domains.definition: Assesses whether GreenOps inputs are actually embedded into planning, engineering, governance and optimisation processes.
domains.why_it_matters: GreenOps maturity is not just about good data. It is about using the data to change decisions and outcomes.
recommendation_themes: Embed GreenOps into existing decision forums
Tie insights to owners and workflow
